title: How to Handle Food Allergies in China as a Tourist
metaDescription: Learn how to safely navigate food allergies while traveling in China. Practical tips, Chinese allergy cards, and emergency resources for tourists.

sections.heading: Prepare an Allergy Card in Mandarin
sections.content: Before your trip, create or download a bilingual allergy card listing your specific allergens in simplified Chinese. Include the severity of your allergy. Services like SelectWisely or Equal Eats offer professionally translated cards. Save a digital copy on your phone and carry a printed version. Show this card to every server before ordering. A clear, direct card is your single most powerful tool.

sections.heading: Know Common Hidden Allergens in Chinese Cuisine
sections.content: Peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish, soy, sesame, wheat, and eggs appear frequently in Chinese cooking. Peanut oil is widely used for stir-frying. Sauces like doubanjiang (chili bean paste) often contain soy and wheat. Many dishes use sesame oil as a finishing ingredient. Cross-contamination in wok cooking is common since kitchens prepare many dishes rapidly. Always ask about ingredients even when a dish looks safe.

sections.heading: Useful Phrases and Communication Tips
sections.content: Learn key phrases like "I am allergic to..." (wǒ duì... guòmǐn) and point to your allergy card confidently. Use translation apps like Baidu Translate for real-time help. In upscale or international restaurants, staff are more likely to understand allergy needs. For casual eateries, bring a local friend or your hotel concierge can help call ahead. Always confirm twice.

sections.heading: Choosing Safer Dining Options
sections.content: Hotels with international breakfast buffets, large restaurant chains, and well-reviewed spots on Dianping (China's top food app) tend to accommodate allergy requests more reliably. Avoid unknown street food if you have severe allergies due to unpredictable ingredients. Consider booking food tours that specialize in dietary restrictions. Self-catering with groceries from supermarkets like Hema or Sam's Club gives you full control.

sections.heading: Emergency Preparedness
sections.content: China's emergency number is 120 for ambulance services. Carry your prescribed epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) at all times and know how to use it. Download the Amap or Baidu Maps app to locate nearby hospitals quickly. Major cities have international hospitals with English-speaking staff. Travel insurance covering allergy-related emergencies is strongly recommended. Keep emergency contacts saved offline on your phone.

keyTakeaways: Always carry a bilingual allergy card in simplified Chinese
Peanut oil, soy, sesame, and shellfish are hidden in many Chinese dishes
Use apps like Baidu Translate and Dianping to communicate and find safe restaurants
Upmarket restaurants and hotel dining are generally safer for allergy sufferers
Dial 120 for emergencies and carry your EpiPen at all times
relatedPhrases: 我对此过敏 (Wǒ duì cǐ guòmǐn) — I am allergic to this
请不要放花生 (Qǐng bùyào fàng huāshēng) — Please do not add peanuts
这个菜有...吗？(Zhège cài yǒu... ma?) — Does this dish contain...?
